map.condition: Need to classify edges (tree/back/forward/cross) in DFS
map.use: \text{Back: } d[u] > d[v],\ \text{Cross: } d[u] < d[v] \text{ (undirected has no cross/forward)}

map.condition: Find time complexity of BFS or DFS on adjacency list

map.use: \text{Kosaraju: 2 DFS passes on } G \text{ and } G^T,\quad O(V+E)
\text{Topo sort: DFS finish-time order (decreasing), or Kahn's BFS with in-degree queue}

map.condition: Count connected components in undirected graph

formulas.name: BFS Time Complexity
formulas.latex: T_{BFS} = O(V + E)
formulas.plain: T_BFS = O(V + E)
formulas.whenToUse: When asked for the time complexity of BFS on an adjacency-list representation.

terms.means: number of vertices
number of edges

formulas.trap: On an adjacency-matrix representation BFS is O(V^2), not O(V+E). Always check the representation.

formulas.name: BFS Uses a Queue
formulas.latex: \text{BFS} \xrightarrow{\text{uses}} \textbf{Queue (FIFO)}
formulas.plain: BFS uses a Queue (FIFO)
formulas.whenToUse: When matching traversal algorithms to their underlying data structures.

terms.means: First-In First-Out; vertices are processed in the order they are discovered

formulas.trap: DFS uses a Stack (or recursion). Swapping stack/queue is a classic GATE distractor.

formulas.name: BFS Shortest Path (Unweighted Graph)
formulas.latex: d[v] = \text{shortest path (in edges) from source } s \text{ to } v
formulas.plain: d[v] = shortest number of edges from source s to v
formulas.whenToUse: When finding shortest paths in an unweighted (or unit-weight) graph.

terms.means: BFS-level / shortest-path distance in hops to v
source vertex

formulas.trap: BFS gives shortest EDGE-count paths; it does not give shortest paths in weighted graphs (use Dijkstra there).

formulas.name: Connected Components via BFS/DFS
formulas.latex: \text{Count SCCs or CCs} \Rightarrow \Theta(m + n)
formulas.plain: Counting connected components using BFS or DFS: Theta(m + n)
formulas.whenToUse: When asked for the most efficient algorithm to count connected components.

terms.means: number of edges
number of vertices

formulas.trap: Theta(mn) or Theta(n^2) are incorrect; the correct complexity is Theta(m+n), not just Theta(m) or Theta(n) alone.

formulas.name: DFS Time Complexity
formulas.latex: T_{DFS} = O(V + E)
formulas.plain: T_DFS = O(V + E)
formulas.whenToUse: When asked for the time complexity of DFS on an adjacency-list representation.

terms.means: number of vertices
number of edges

formulas.trap: O(V+E) holds only for adjacency-list; adjacency-matrix DFS is O(V^2).

formulas.name: DFS Uses a Stack
formulas.latex: \text{DFS} \xrightarrow{\text{uses}} \textbf{Stack (LIFO)}
formulas.plain: DFS uses a Stack (LIFO) — or implicit call stack via recursion
formulas.whenToUse: When matching traversal algorithms to their underlying data structures.

terms.means: Last-In First-Out; the most recently discovered unexplored vertex is processed first

formulas.trap: BFS uses a queue, not a stack. State-space DFS expands nodes in alphabetical/numerical order when specified.

formulas.name: DFS Discovery and Finish Timestamps
formulas.latex: d[v] < f[v], \quad d[v] < d[u] < f[u] < f[v] \Rightarrow u \text{ is a descendant of } v
formulas.plain: d[v] < f[v]; if d[v] < d[u] < f[u] < f[v] then u is a descendant of v in DFS tree
formulas.whenToUse: When analyzing DFS tree structure or classifying edges using discovery/finish times.

terms.means: discovery time of vertex v
finish time of vertex v

formulas.trap: In a directed graph, cross edges go from higher d[u] to lower d[v] (already finished), not the other way around.

formulas.name: Parenthesis Theorem
formulas.latex: [d[u], f[u]] \text{ and } [d[v], f[v]] \text{ are either disjoint or one contains the other}
formulas.plain: Intervals [d[u],f[u]] and [d[v],f[v]] are either disjoint or nested (never partially overlapping)
formulas.whenToUse: When reasoning about ancestor/descendant relationships in DFS.

terms.means: discovery and finish times of u
discovery and finish times of v

formulas.trap: Partial overlap of intervals is impossible in a valid DFS — if you compute one, you made an error tracing the DFS.

formulas.name: Tree Edge
formulas.latex: (u, v) \text{ is a tree edge if } v \text{ is first discovered via } u
formulas.plain: Tree edge (u,v): v is first visited while exploring u
formulas.whenToUse: When labeling DFS tree edges in both directed and undirected graphs.

terms.means: parent vertex in DFS tree
child vertex, freshly discovered

formulas.trap: Tree edges form the DFS spanning tree; not all edges of the graph are tree edges.

formulas.name: Back Edge
formulas.latex: (u, v): d[v] < d[u] \text{ and } v \text{ is an ancestor of } u \Rightarrow \text{back edge}
formulas.plain: Back edge (u,v): v is an ancestor of u in DFS tree; implies a cycle
formulas.whenToUse: When detecting cycles in directed graphs or classifying DFS edges.

terms.means: discovery time of ancestor v
discovery time of u (greater than d[v])

formulas.trap: In undirected graphs, there are only tree edges and back edges (no forward or cross edges).

formulas.name: Forward and Cross Edges (Directed Graphs Only)
formulas.latex: \text{Forward: } (u,v),\ d[u] < d[v],\ v \text{ descendant of } u \\ \text{Cross: } (u,v),\ f[v] < d[u] \text{ (v already finished)}
formulas.plain: Forward edge: (u,v) where v is a descendant but not a child; Cross edge: (u,v) where v is already finished
formulas.whenToUse: When classifying edges in a directed graph DFS.

terms.means: finish time of v
discovery time of u

formulas.trap: Cross and forward edges do NOT exist in undirected DFS. GATE often gives undirected graphs and asks for all four types — only tree and back exist.

formulas.name: Cycle Detection via DFS
formulas.latex: \text{Directed graph has a cycle} \Leftrightarrow \text{DFS finds a back edge}
formulas.plain: A directed graph has a cycle if and only if DFS discovers a back edge
formulas.whenToUse: When determining if a directed graph is a DAG (no cycle = no back edge).

terms.means: edge from a vertex to one of its ancestors in the DFS tree

formulas.trap: In undirected graphs, every non-tree edge is a back edge, so the condition still works but every undirected graph with >= V edges has a cycle.

formulas.name: Kosaraju's SCC Algorithm
formulas.latex: \text{Step 1: DFS on } G \text{ — record finish times} \\ \text{Step 2: DFS on } G^T \text{ in decreasing finish-time order}
formulas.plain: Kosaraju: (1) DFS on G to get finish order; (2) DFS on G-transpose in reverse finish-time order; each DFS tree in step 2 is one SCC
formulas.whenToUse: When finding all SCCs of a directed graph.

terms.means: transpose (reverse) of G: all edge directions flipped
maximal set of vertices where every vertex is reachable from every other

formulas.trap: Both DFS passes are needed; skipping the transpose step gives wrong SCCs.

formulas.name: SCC Algorithm Time Complexity
formulas.latex: T_{\text{SCC}} = O(V + E)
formulas.plain: SCC (Kosaraju or Tarjan) runs in O(V + E)
formulas.whenToUse: When asked for the complexity of finding all SCCs.

terms.means: vertices
edges

formulas.trap: O(VE) or O(V^2) are common wrong answers; correct complexity is O(V+E).

formulas.name: SCC Condensation Graph
formulas.latex: \text{Condensation of } G = \text{DAG of SCCs}
formulas.plain: Condensing G by collapsing each SCC to a single node yields a DAG
formulas.whenToUse: When reasoning about the high-level structure of a directed graph after SCC decomposition.

terms.means: directed graph where nodes are SCCs and edges represent cross-SCC edges in G

formulas.trap: The condensation is always a DAG; if you get a cycle in the condensation you have merged too few vertices into one SCC.

formulas.name: Topological Ordering Condition
formulas.latex: \text{For every edge } (u, v) \in E, \quad u \text{ appears before } v \text{ in the ordering}
formulas.plain: Topological order: for every directed edge (u,v), u comes before v
formulas.whenToUse: When verifying whether a given sequence is a valid topological ordering of a DAG.

terms.means: directed edge from u to v

formulas.trap: Topological sort is only defined for DAGs. A graph with a cycle has no valid topological ordering.

formulas.name: DFS-Based Topological Sort
formulas.latex: \text{Topo order} = \text{vertices sorted by decreasing finish time } f[v]
formulas.plain: Topological sort = vertices in decreasing order of DFS finish time f[v]
formulas.whenToUse: When performing topological sort using DFS.

terms.means: DFS finish time of vertex v

formulas.trap: It is DECREASING finish time, not increasing. Reversing the finish order is the final step.

formulas.name: Kahn's Algorithm (BFS Topological Sort)
formulas.latex: \text{Enqueue all vertices with in-degree 0; dequeue and reduce in-degrees; repeat}
formulas.plain: Kahn's: enqueue all in-degree-0 vertices; dequeue u, output u, decrement in-degree of all neighbors; re-enqueue when in-degree hits 0
formulas.whenToUse: When performing topological sort iteratively or when also checking for cycles (queue empties before all vertices processed => cycle exists).

terms.means: number of incoming edges to a vertex

formulas.trap: If the queue empties before all V vertices are output, the graph has a cycle and no topological order exists.

formulas.name: Topological Sort Time Complexity
formulas.latex: T_{\text{topo}} = O(V + E)
formulas.plain: Topological sort (DFS or Kahn's) runs in O(V + E)
formulas.whenToUse: When asked for the complexity of topological sorting.

terms.means: vertices
edges

formulas.trap: O(V log V) is the comparison-sort lower bound, not the graph topo-sort bound. Topo sort is linear O(V+E).

formulas.name: DAG iff No Back Edge in DFS
formulas.latex: G \text{ is a DAG} \Leftrightarrow \text{DFS finds no back edge}
formulas.plain: G is a DAG (directed acyclic graph) if and only if DFS finds no back edge
formulas.whenToUse: When checking whether a given directed graph is a DAG.

terms.means: Directed Acyclic Graph — a directed graph with no directed cycles

formulas.trap: Forward and cross edges are fine in a DAG; only back edges indicate a cycle.

formulas.name: Undirected DFS Edge Types
formulas.latex: \text{Undirected DFS: only } \textbf{Tree edges} \text{ and } \textbf{Back edges}
formulas.plain: In undirected DFS, every edge is either a tree edge or a back edge — no forward or cross edges
formulas.whenToUse: When classifying edges in undirected graph DFS.

terms.means: edge that extends the DFS tree
edge to an ancestor in the DFS tree

formulas.trap: Cross edges exist in directed DFS only. GATE questions sometimes show an undirected graph and ask about all four types — forward and cross cannot occur.

formulas.name: BFS Can Find Connected Components
formulas.latex: \text{BFS (and DFS) can both find connected components of an undirected graph in } O(V+E)
formulas.plain: BFS can find connected components; the claim that BFS cannot find connected components is FALSE
formulas.whenToUse: When answering true/false questions about BFS vs DFS capabilities.

terms.means: maximal subgraph where every pair of vertices is connected by a path

formulas.trap: A GATE 2007 question presented 'BFS cannot find connected components' as a choice — this is FALSE. Both BFS and DFS find connected components.

formulas.name: DFS Spanning Tree
formulas.latex: \text{DFS on connected graph } G \Rightarrow \text{spanning tree } T \subseteq G
formulas.plain: DFS on a connected graph produces a spanning tree T of G
formulas.whenToUse: When asked about DFS tree properties or about vertices adjacent in the DFS tree.

terms.means: DFS spanning tree — subgraph containing all vertices, connected by tree edges

formulas.trap: If v is the first new vertex visited after u in the DFS tree, v is a child of u (not just any neighbor). Being adjacent in G does not mean being adjacent in the DFS tree.
